coredata.dc:title: Cu-CDs as dual optical and electrochemical nanosensor for βME detection
coredata.dc:creator: Behboudi, Hamidreza
Pourmadadi, Mehrab
Omidi, Meisam
Rahmandoust, Moones
Siadat, Seyed Omid Ranaei
Shayeh, Javad Shabani
coredata.dc:description: Thiols play critical roles in biological processes. Here, a facile hydrothermal synthesis approach was used for fabrication of flourescent Cu-carbon dots (Cu-CDs), to be employed as accurate thiol Nanosensors. The obtained Cu-CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy, UV–visible, photoluminescence and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy scannings. The highest-occupied- and lowest-unoccupied- molecular-orbitals and the pertaining energy band gap were achieved using analytical chemistry density functional theory. Electrochemical performance of Cu-CDs showed the capability of the fabricred nanoparticles for accurate, sensitive, and selective nanosensing of thiols, as both electrochemical and optical detection tools. The electrochemical peak of Cu-CDs were selectively, rationally and intensively suppressed by thiols, due to the strong electrostatic interaction and covalent bonding between the NH2 functional groups of the Cu-CDs and -SH groups of thiols. On the other hand, the blue photoluminescence emission of Cu-CDs rationally increased, due to detachment of Cu ions from the edges of Cu-CDs in the existance of thiols. According to the above description, the limit of detections (LODs) of 1 nM was achieved, introducing such-prepared Cu-CDs nanosensors, as simple, fast, low-cost, sensitive and selective tools for thiol detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Metal CDs
Nanosensor
Thiol detection
Electrochemistry
Photoluminescence
Fluorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a: UV/Vis absorption spectra. b: Emission spectra of the CDs in excitation wavelengths between 270 and 600 nm, c: FTIR and d: TEM image, with inset DLS data.
XPS results, a,b: binind energies, c: C1, d: N1 and e: O1.
a–c: CV of Cu-CDs solution in pH 6–8, including different concentrations of βME.
a: CV diagram at different concentrations of Cu-CDs; The calibration carve of the influence of b: scan rate, and c: Cu-CD concentration on PL emission.
a: The CV diagram at different concentrations of βME. b: The corrsponding calibration carve.
Increasing of the amount of PL emission of Cu-CDs by increase of βME concentration.
Schematic diagram of the fluoresent nanosensor's functioning procedure.
Distribution of charge on CDs, befor and after incorporation of Cu ions.
CV of the Cu-CDs when introduced to 10 nM of a:Benzo thiophen b: Carbon disulfide c: Urea.
PL and CV digrams of the sensor's respond in various βME concentration in PBS.
Linear range at various Cu-CD concentrations.

head.ce:title: Cu-CDs as dual optical and electrochemical nanosensor for βME detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Protein Research Center, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Protein Research Center, Shahid Beheshti University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Protein Research Center, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thiols play critical roles in biological processes. Here, a facile hydrothermal synthesis approach was used for fabrication of flourescent Cu-carbon dots (Cu-CDs), to be employed as accurate thiol Nanosensors. The obtained Cu-CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy, UV–visible, photoluminescence and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy scannings. The highest-occupied- and lowest-unoccupied- molecular-orbitals and the pertaining energy band gap were achieved using analytical chemistry density functional theory. Electrochemical performance of Cu-CDs showed the capability of the fabricred nanoparticles for accurate, sensitive, and selective nanosensing of thiols, as both electrochemical and optical detection tools. The electrochemical peak of Cu-CDs were selectively, rationally and intensively suppressed by thiols, due to the strong electrostatic interaction and covalent bonding between the NH2 functional groups of the Cu-CDs and -SH groups of thiols. On the other hand, the blue photoluminescence emission of Cu-CDs rationally increased, due to detachment of Cu ions from the edges of Cu-CDs in the existance of thiols. According to the above description, the limit of detections (LODs) of 1 nM was achieved, introducing such-prepared Cu-CDs nanosensors, as simple, fast, low-cost, sensitive and selective tools for thiol detection.

ce:section.ce:para: In the recent decade, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant attention because of their unique optical and electrical properties, stable emission, resistance to photobleaching, chemical inertness, aqueous solubility, well dispersibility, and easy functionalization, which make them interesting materials for development of novel nano-biosensor [1,2]. Since 2012, the hydrothermal technique for synthesis of CDs has been considered as a facile and low-cost chemical synthesis method [3], which provides opportunity for modifying and functionalazing the surface of CDs with various chemicals, for different applications [4].

Thiols, on the other hand, are important molecules to be detected in the human's body, in biological processes, and in the environment [5,6]. In this family, the mercapto biomolecules, for instance, play critical roles in maintaining of biological processes. Cysteine, homocysteine, glutathione and hydrogen sulfide are other members of thiol family that perform crucial functions in various biological processes [7,8].

Alternation in the level of cysteine concentration, for example, was observed in many syndromes and diseases, such as, growth failure in children, decreased levels of serum essential proteins, edema, lethargy, loss of pigmentation in hair, muscle loss, fat loss, and weakness [9,10]. Lack of cysteine can cause decreased production of glutathione, as the most abundant low molecular weight non-protein biothiol of the intracellular environment. Glutathione performs many of cell functions, such as alleviate cellular oxidative stress, detoxifying of xenobiotic or of cells for maintaining functions of hepatics cells by neutralizing free radicals in the liver or on all tissues and organs in human body. It is also responsible for intracellular signal transduction, as well as maintaining redox homeostasis, and gene regulation [11,12]. The deficiency of the glutathione may also be resulted from the immune suppression, related to HIV [12,13], and in general, variations in the level of biothiols in the human body may be due to different diseases, such as liver damage, leucocyte loss, and almost all cancers [14–16]. Thus, the detection of thiols, with high selectivity, is vital in fast disease diagnosis.

Among various sensors, fluorescent and calorimetric reaction based CD probes have been very useful in the detection of thiols. The technique includes using fluorescent labeling agents, which are based on electrostatic interaction and covalent bonds between the -SH groups of thiols and the surface functional groups of CDs [13]. Compared to other thiol detection methods such as absorption-and-detection, nanoparticle-based fluorescent probes show higher sensitivity with the limit of detections in the range of nano- and pico-molar, due to the higher ratio of the surface to the volume in nanoparticles [17,18]. In addition, the absorption methods, especially in the UV range, are more prone to interference with the biological environment and/or the substrate [19]. However, fluorescent probes have the disadvantage of the lack of selectivity between different biological thiols. Indeed cysteine, homocysteine, and glutathione have similar structures and hence, successful detection and quantification of specific biothiols using fluorescent labeling methods depend very much on the employed separations technique, such as high pressure liquid chromatography (HPLC), capillary zone electrophoresis, or cross-entropy (CE) to differentiate various biothiol derivatives [19,20]. Although various methods have been used for the selective determination of different biothiols, utilization of these analytical instruments make the detection process time-consuming, and it may incur higher costs, and necessity of expert laboratory operation [21]. Therefore, there was a trend toward using easy-to-use nanosensors, based on electrochemical and photoluminescence methods, which are cost-effective and have high sensitivity, selectivity and ease of miniaturization for point-of-care detection of biothiols [22].

Electrochemical sensors, with the possibility of having their electrode surfaces modified for enhanced sensitivity, have found broad applications in small molecule sensing, including sensitive determination of biothiols. Different nanomaterials were used as excellent electrochemical electrodes, according to their reliable electrochemical activity [23,24], from which CDs possess desirable merits in most of the electrochemical applications [25,26].

In the year 2015, Wang et al. developed nanohybrids of AuNPs embedded within magnetite-porous carbon shell NPs (Fe3O4@PC-CDs) for cellular imaging and photothermal therapy [27]. In another study conducted by Jana et al., a nanocomposites of AgNPs and N, S-CDs were synthesized via reduction of AgNO3 solution at room temperature. The prepared nanocomposites were used to evaluate their sensing properties [28]. Si-CDs were fabricated from glycerol via microwave irradiation and were conjugated by dopamine (DA) via simple mixing in water. They investigated the as-prepared DA@Si-CDs as an intracellular Ag+ nanosensor [29]. More recently, Chen et al. synthesized MnO2-CDs nanocomposites by reduction of KMnO4 at room temperature via CDs from polythiophene. They used the obtained nanocomposite as a pH/H2O2-responsive nanosensor [30].

In addition to sensing of different molecules and agents, Using metal-CDs as a bio-imaging agents and nanosensor was also found to be very promising. metal-CDs can be used as bio-imaging agents. Initially, a porous Fe3O4@Ag-CDs was synthesized by Wang et al. They used ferrocene, hydrogen peroxide, and AgNO3 to obtain Metal-CDs with superior fluorescence properties, and used them as a bio-imaging agent to detect mouse melanoma cell lines [31]. Then, Hg2+ nanosensor was developed by Zhang et al. via hydrothermal treatment of Adenosine disodium triphosphate as a source of CDs. They used lanthanum for coating CDs to obtain a metal-CDs bioimaging agent and also a Hg2+ nanosensor [32]. Hengwei Lin employed metal-CDs are screened and designed as a triple-channel fluorescent nanosensor for the identification of various phosphate anions (e.g., ATP, ADP, AMP, PPi, and Pi). According to their findings, the quenched fluorescence of CDs by metal ions such as Fe3+and Cu2+, can be recovered by adding phosphate anions to metal-CDs solution. The phenomenon is due to the existance of various number of phosphate group and steric hindrance effects [33]. A nitrogen and Zn co-doped CDs (N, Zn-CDs) with high fluorescence property was synthesized by Das et al., which showed excellent luminescence and average fluorescence lifetime. N, Zn-CDs were further used as a biosensor for labeling E. coli [34]. Finally, a nanosensor for Cr(VI) detecting was developed by Qing et al. via Cu2+ doped CDs. They fabricated Cu2+-CDs using tea extract as a source of carbon via hydrothermal method. Furthermore, Cr(VI) sensing potential of Cu2+-CDs were thoroughly investigated [35]. Therefore in this report, Cu-functionalized CD (Cu-CD)/glassy carbon electrodes are prepared for sensitive electrochemical detection of thiol molecules, as important indicators of various diseases in human body

All electrochemical experiments were performed using Ivium potentiostat (Netherlands) and the resulting curves were recorded using the Ivium software (IviumSoft version 1.9). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis was carried out in a conventional three-electrode cell of a glassy carbon electrode, an Ag/AgCl and a platinum plate as the working, reference and counter electrodes, respectively. Fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan). The absorption spectra were recorded by a Hitachi UV-2910 spectrophotometer (Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometry was performed by a Nicolet Nexus 670 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were carried out using a Perkin Elmer PHI 5702 XPS spectrometer (USA). The sizes of Cu-CDs were characterized using dynamic light scattering (DLS, Hitachi-600, Japan). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV was applied.

All the chemical materials used in this study, such as CuCl2.2H2O, diammonium hydrogen citrate, 2-Mercaptoethanol (βME) and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Merck Chemical Co. (Germany), with analytical grade and were used without further purification. High purity double-distilled water was used throughout the experiments. The Gibco™ fetal bovine serum (FBS) (Thermo Fisher, USA) was used for real sample analysis.

First, 2 gr of diammonium hydrogen citrate were added to 75 ml of double distilled water. Then the solution was placed on the heater-stirrer to dissolve completely. At the next step, the solution was poured into a stainless steel autoclave and was placed in the oven at 150 °C for 18 h. After completion of the reaction, the vessel was allowed to naturally cool down to obtain a bright yellow solution, indicating the formation of carbon dots. The solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 20 min and then filtered using a dialysis membrane (3000 Da) in 2 l of deionized water for 4 h. At the end, CDs with concentration of 26.66 mg/ml were obtained.

At the next stage, 10 ml of CDs with concentration of 2 mg ml−1 were diluted, then the solution was mixed with 10 ml of CuCl2.2H2O and placed on heater-stirrer for 3 h to obtain a homogenous solution. Then, 10 mg of NaBH4 was added to the solution as the reducer and then stirred for 2 h at 27°C. The solution final color was light pink.
CuCl2·2H2O (s) + 2NaOH (s) → 2NaCl (aq) +Cu(OH)2 (aq) + 2H2O

The measurement was accomplished in 5 ml of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS buffer) environment (10 mM, pH:7.4). Cu-CDs were used as a redox probe. First, the glassy carbon working electrode was polished with 0.05 μM alumina slurry, and then it was ultrasonically cleaned in high purity double distilled water and absolute ethanol (purity > 99%). Cu-CDs was added to the PBS buffer solution and CV was measured for each concentration.

The quantum yield (Φ) of the synthetized Cu-CDs was calculated by a single point equation scheme, using quinine sulfate as the reference which has a quantum yield of 8% at 360 nm excitation wavelength. The below-mentioned equation was used to calculate the Cu-CDs quantum yield:
Φ
=
Φ
s t
(
m /
m
s t
)
(
η 2
/
η
s t
2
)
where the Φ is the quantum yield of Cu-CDs, and the Φst is the standard reference fluorophore's quantum yield that has a defined value, m is the slope of the integrated photoluminescence intensities at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, and η is the refractive index of the solvent. For calculation of the Cu-CDs quantum yield, six concentrations of both of Cu-CDs and quinine sulfate were made, all of the samples had an absorbance wavelength less than 0.1 nm at 360 nm. The obtained data was plotted to assure good linearity. To minimizing the reabsorption effects, the absorbance in the 1 cm quartz fluorescence cuvettes was kept below 0.1 at the 360 nm excitation wavelength. The excitation slit width was set to be 1.0 nm, whereas the emission slit width was 2.5 nm.

Quinine sulfate concentrations were dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid (refractive index (η) of 1.33), while the Cu-CDs samples were dissolved in water (η = 1.33). The Cu-CDs and the quinine sulfate fluorescence spectra at similar excitation wavelength of 360 nm were recorded. Next, by comparing the integrated photoluminescence intensities at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the absorption of the carbon dots sample with the quinine sulfate as the reference, Φ of the Cu-CDs was defined. The quantum yield for Cu-CDs is found to be 8%.

The standard addition method was used to test the applicability of the biosensor. Concentrations of 1, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 μM cysteine were added to the diluted FBS. All the analytes were mixed with Cu-CDs. The electrochemical CV and photoluminescence studies were then performed on the samples.

The HOMO and LUMO states of the molecular orbital of pristine Cu-CD molecule was modeled using commercial Gaussian 09 W software, version 9.5 and was copper saturated on edges. CDs were first modeled in a perfect hexagonal state, and then the energy of the designed model was minimized using Brenner function potential, leading to inevitable dislocations in atom sites. The frontier molecular orbitals and the pertaining energy gap of CDs were then obtained then by employing density functional theory calculations using the B3LYP/6–31 G basis set.

The UV/Vis absorption spectra of Cu-CDs were analyzed, which showed a dominant peak in the range of 270 nm (Fig. 1 a). The synthesized particle shows a high potential for use in biological applications, which is why stability is present. Fig. 1b shows the emission spectra of the nanoparticle in an excitation between 270 and 600 nm wavelengths, with a maximum emission at 440 nm. This maximum emission was used to obtain quantum yield of Cu-CDs. Rhodamine 6 G was the standard criteria, which obtained Quantum Yield of 8%. Impressively, a linear relationship was obtained between the wavelengths of 320 nm–600 nm and CDs had high fluorescence emission.

As shown in Fig. 1d, Cu-CDs have a spherical morphology and a 4–6 nm size, as confirmed by DLS. The average nanoparticle size is 4.25 nm. Subsequently, the emission of fluorescence nanoparticles was investigated at pH values of 6 to 8, indicating that the emission was stable at the indicated pH. In FTIR studies (Fig. 1c), the dominant peaks in the range 3434–3122 cm−1 correspond to N-H, -OH, respectively. The peak of 1637 cm−1 is related to the C=O bond. 1420 and 1162 cm−1 peaks correspond to C-N and C-O, respectively.

The data obtained from the FTIR are confirmed by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data. Fig. 2 a and d confirm the existance of the oxidized carbon on the surface of the nanoparticle. Based on the C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2c), carbon atoms exist in two different groups of C-C and O-C=O. Existence of a large amount of oxygen ion on the surface of nanoparticles shows a high degree of passivation in Cu-CDs. In this binding range, the peak of the amine group N-C=O was visible in the range of 370 eV. The three C1s peaks were in the range of 264.2, 265.8 and 267.1 eV, which are related to the C=C/C-C, C-N and C=O/C-OH groups, respectively. Fig. 2e shows the Cu-CDs O1s spectrum, which had two peaks in the range of 488.5 and 490 eV, related to the C=O and C-OH/COC groups. The N1s spectrum in Fig. 2d has N-C-N and N-H groups, which were associated with peaks of 369.2 and 375 eV, respectively. Regarding surface modification and doping of Cu2+ions in the nanoparticle, the Cu2p spectrum was investigated with relatively good intensity peaks in the range of 797.3 and 804.4 eV, indicating the presence of oxidized Cu+ ions in the nanoparticle composition (Fig. 2b). Relatively similar results are obtained for the Cu2+ ions in the CD structure. Also, poor peaks in the 804.4 range indicated the presence of very low amounts of Cu2+ ions in nanoparticles, which may be due to higher Cu+ reactivity than Cu2+.

Test of reproducibility for this nanosensor in different pH in the range of 6–8 was performed in three pH points. Many reports indicate that pH alterations change PL emission of the carbon dots. So, we studied the influence of pH on the PL emission of Cu-CDs, with and without the extistance of βME (0, 1, and 10 nM). With the pH alteration, not only the PL emission was influenced, but it altered the CV peak in electrochemistry, leading to creation of new peaks (Fig. 3 ). This fact also proved that this nanosensor could detect and reproduce thiols groups in this pH range. The most appropriate pH range with the least changes in the Cu-CD electrochemical peaks was at pH from 6 to 8. In acidic pHs lower than 6, because of high protonation of βME groups, the sensitivity of the sensor decreased. On the other hand, in pHs higher than 8, because of the electrostatic interaction between OH- groups in the solution with Cu ions and formation of Cu(OH)2, a change in electrochemistry peak was witnessed. Therefore, PBS solution with pH = 7.4 was opted for measurements.

The interaction between βME and Cu is very fast. When βME is dded to the solution, the interaction occurs after 30 s at room temperature. After that electrochemical peak decreases and fluorescence increases. So we need to do the measurements after 30 s. According to the results, an increase in temperature, decreases the time required for the interaction and it can reduce the time to less than 30 s. Also, as time goes by, nonradiative decay processes lead to a reduction in PL relaxation decay in CDs. In a report, the decrease in fluorescence of carbon dot was analyzed under the influence of temperature increase [36]. It was concluded that with an increase in temperature from 77 °K to 300 °K PL emission was decreased four times. Since PL decreases with temperature increase, all the analyses were performed at 25 °C, i.e. room temperature.

The influence of Cu-CDs’ concentration on βME measurement was analyzed. As suggested by electrochemistry studies, the highest rate of current change was in Cu-CDs’ concentration between 350 and 550 μM. the most significant peak changes for Cu-CDs were obtained for a concentrations of about 400 μM. Therefore, 400 μM concentration of Cu-CDs was used to control the sensitivity of the nanosensor and all of the data was taken in 400 μM concentration, hereafter. Diffusion control analysis for Cu-CDs in 10–150 mV/s of scan rate was performed, and I-peak diagram (Fig. 4 b) was drawn according to the square root scan rate (R2 = 0.9997).

In the case of PL, as shown in Fig. 4c and discussed in Table 1 , the influence Cu-CD concentration on the PL emission was studied. When the concentration of Cu-CDs was very low (50 μM) both PL emission and linear range were low. But with an increase in Cu-CD concentration, an increase in the PL and its linear range was witnesse.

However, since with increasing the Cu-CD concentration higher amounts of βME would be required to influence the PL emission, with high concentrations the Cu-CDs, the measurement precision would decreased. So, for the determination of the appropriate density of Cu-CDs the two variables of PL emission and linear range were both taken into account and the concentration of 200 μM of Cu-CDs was selected as the optimum, to provide good fluorescent intensity and proper linear range. According to these results, the optimum pH, temperature, and concentration was selected to study βME concentrations between 1 and 100 μM.

Electrochemical detection has an significant advantage over other detection methods, such as fluorescence and spectroscopic methods, as long as the measurement is perform directly at the electrode surface without a need for any additional derivatisation prior to separation of the sample. According to Fig. 5 a, the intensity of the maximum current rate peak (MAX Peak), which is also known as I-peak in CV diagram, was directly related to the increasing of Cu-CDs’ concentration. Fig. 5b indicates that the increase in the intensity of MAX Peak in CV is related to increasing the concentration of Cu-CDs, with an appropriate and logarithmic linearity (R2 = 0.9971).

The oxidation current of the cyclic voltammetry wave at 0.00 V recorded at Cu-CDs/glassy carbon electrode is ten times higher than that at bare glassy carbon electrode. It is supposed, in addition to the strong interaction of Cu-CDs and thiols, that Cu-CDs with small size and high surface to volume also contribute to the great catalytic activity of CDs. Electrochemical detection of biothiols at low and zero oxidation potential is assuring, which may be due to the regular interfering components that would not be oxidized at low and zero potential. Measurement in a potential close to zero led to lack of intervention of the ions like OH with the measured samples in the solution. Experiments show that the existence of big cations influences V greatly via increasing cathodic transient potential. The influence of cations is much more than anions like Na or Li. Consequently, measurement in a potential close to zero decreases the cation-anion interaction to the minimum and improves the results, consequently. Therefore, the designed nanosensor not only shows excellent detection in μM range concentration of βME thiols, but also it shows promising sensitivity at zero potential.

In fact, Cu functionalization enables CDs to interact more specifically with thiol molecules on the electrode surface, through the strong electrostatic interaction between thiols and Cu-CDs at zero potential. It subsequently leads to sensitive detection of thiols, due to stable interaction between the functional groups of the Cu-CDs and -SH groups of thiols. The most important point about this nanosensor is the electrochemical detection of biothiols at low and zero oxidation potential. The MAX peak related to Cu-CDs in about zero potential, not only causes less damage to nanosensor, but also it assures that at low and zero potential the regular interfering components would not be oxidized. Based on this fact, this nanosensor exhibits superiority compared to other thiols sensor [37–40].

In order to compute the sensor's average sensitivity via measuring the chart's line slop, changes of current intensity peak according to the concentration are taken into account. Average sensitivity for this nanosensor for βME concentration in the range of 1–100 μM was 0.97. Comparing with similar electrochemical nanosensors which were used to measurement thiols with other methods and technique, this amount of sensitivity is appropriate. The thiol compounds provide well developed catalytic signals, which are proportionate to their concentration. The mentioned progress facilitates the determination of nanomolar concentrations.

Photoluminescence is the phenomenon of visible electromagnetic emission (light) after photon absorption. It is one of the many forms of luminescence, initiated through photon-excitation. The period between absorption and emission may vary from femtosecond to up to minutes or hours, under special conditions. The source of the detected optoelectronic demeanor is an issue of discourse to date. However, there have been plenty of efforts concentrated on the physicochemical features of CDs. several reasons were proposed as the source of PL in CDs including the quantum confinement effect, which leads to recombination of electron-hole pairs in small sp2 carbon clusters on an sp3 matrix, and the effect of the surface states, which is fueled by different functional groups and defects. More interestingly, the CDs PL can be efficiently changed by electron donor or electron acceptor molecules in the analysis environment, making it a valuable fluorescence probe for sensing of metal ions.

Coating of CDs with some metals like Hg and Cu decreases the PL of CDs significantly, toward quenching of the PL emission. Many factors including carbon source, metal ion density, temperature, and interaction time affect PL. An increase in copper concentration, in this case, from 0.15 (the efficient density to get the appropriate PL) to 0.75 decreased PL significantly. This is because of the blockage of the passivated surface defect. The energy level of doped copper is less than the excited state of the CDs. So, the agitated electrons of CDs create weaker PL emission when they enter the energy level of copper. Also, a study by Depeng Kong et al. indicated that the PL of CDs is quenched totally because of the formation of a nonfluorescent metal complex between the chelating oxygen atoms on the surface of the carbon dots and Cu and Pb ions [41]. Also, another report stated that paramagnetic ions of Cu and Pb have an unfilled d-shell that can quench the PL of carbonic nanomaterials based on electron or energy transfer mechanism [42]. The quenching of the PL emission might also be due to the aggregation of metal CDs as a result of their interaction with surface functional groups of other CDs. Sulfhydryl groups, for instance, are known for their great tendency towards metal ions. In this case, upon photon excitation, CDs are electron donators for metal reduction in aqueous environments and this excitation deposits the metals on the surface of the CDs. Hence, coated or doped metal ions quench the PL emission even in small amounts. This quenching may be because of the disorders in electron-hole radiative recombination, which is in charge of PL emission in CDs.

Here, the PL intensity variations of fluorescent probe (Cu-CDs) vs. the different concentrations of βME, as a repesenter of thiol -SH group were investigated. As expected, about 30 s after adding the thiol to the Cu-CDs solution, the PL intensity of Cu-CDs solution increased rationally upon increasing the concentration of βME from 1 to 100 μM (Fig. 6 ). In fact, the PL emission of Cu-CDs was recovered when thiol's -SH groups were introduced to the Cu-CDs’ aqueous nanosensing system, due to the strong affinity between Cu-CDs and thiols.

The fluorescent sensor perfomes better especially at lower concentrations of βME, i.e. below 2 μM, as Fig. 5a and b both admit. According to the above description, the nanosensor for detecting of thiols can be achieved easily, and gives us the limit of detections (LODs) of 1 nM.

Fig. 7 schematically shows the procedure of the fluorescent sensing platform. From top left the CDs are shown after they are synthetized. Then, CuCl2 is introduced to modify the surface of the CDs and create the Cu-CDs. At this step, the fluorescent of CDs dramaticaly decreases. At the third stage, when βME is introduced to the analysis environment with increasing concentrations, the thiol groups absorb copper ions, separating them from the carboxyl groups on the edges of the Cu-CDs. As a result of this interaction, the quenching effect of the metals is terminated and a PL emission increase is observed, as confirmed by the HUMO & LUMO simulations.

The molecular orbitals and their properties, such as energy and frontier electron density, are important for determining the reactive positions. Hence, values of frontier molecular orbital energies of HOMO and LUMO states, as the electron donor and acceptor orbitals, respectively, as well as their corresponding band gap were obtained for CDs to reflect the chemical activity of the molecule, reporting a homogenous distribution of the density of states on both zigzag and armchair edges of the CDs, separately. The results also show the characteristic zero-band-gap nature of CD, with approximately ΔE = 0.03 eV, as calculated using 6–31 G basis set.

After adding Cu ions, because of the existance of a great amount of amino-groups in the center of the CDs, molecular dynamic simulation data indicates that the majority of the metal ions are placed on the edges, influencing the distribution of charge on CDs and hence, quenching their PL emission (Fig. 8 ). As βME is introduced to the CDs analysis environment with a concentration of 100 μM, since thiol groups have a great tendency to copper, it is separated from the carboxyl surface of the CDs and as it exits the interaction and connects to thiol groups. Then, as the doped copper is separated from the surface of the CDs the quenching effect of the metals is terminated and fluorescence emission increase occurs. HUMO & LUMO simulation proved this processes. According to the results of using the optimum pH and temperature, adding amounts of βME between 1 and 100 μM, the fluorescent probe demonstrated sensitivity and the linear range. In this concentration range, by adding βME and formation of Cu-mercapto and as a result releasing of the complex from the surface of the CDs increases the fluorescence of the CDs slowly.

To make sure that thiols are responsible for the increased PL and the decreased current peak, maleimide was used as a thiol blocking agent. Repeated tests with maleimide-treated sample confirmed no change in PL nor in the CV peak of electrochemistry, which proved the selectivity test of the fluorescent probes for thiols. Furthermore, selectivity test was performed using 10 nM concentration for carbon disulfide, benzothiophene and urea were used in 400 μM concentration of Cu-CDs in PBS buffer solution (10 nM, pH = 7.4). Results demonstrated that nanosensor does not show any reaction to these materials and the intensity of peak MAX for CV related to Cu-CDs and also the surface under the graph for CV did not show any change. It can be concluded from above observations that the nanosensor react to thiols groups and have excellent selectivity for thiols groups (Fig. 9 ).

In order to indicate appropriate functioning of the designed sensor, βME in FBS was studied. Fetal bovine serum was used to test the effectiveness of the sensor in real biological environment. FBS was diluted with PBS to guarantee that the density of the thiols is in the linear range. Then, a standard addition method with cysteine was done as a standard to analyze the concentration of the thiols in FBS. As expected, adding FBS to the environment increased the amount of the PL. An interesting fact was that the electrochemistry method was very efficient, in this case. The close to zero potential measurements on the CV peak led to the least effect of the biological specimens.

Like all the other tests performed by βME, the designed sensor was capable to function, when cysteine was introduced. The optimum circumstances of the experiment were used to determine the density of cysteine, as well. As expected, adding cysteine concentrations from 1 to 50 μM changed the peaks in a linear range in both electrochemistry and PL sensors (Fig. 10 ).

In this investigation, a simple, credible and high selectivity method was introduced to measure the amount of thiols in biological samples using electrochemistry and PL methods by Cu-CDs. In the presence of biothiols, the reduced PL of Cu-CDs, due to the non-fluorescent metal complex between copper and CD surface groups, increases significantly, as a result of the interaction between copper ion and the biothiols. Also, electrochemistry peaks showed a remarkable decrease. The changes in electrochemistry and PL were the range of nM and μM. The limit of detection was achieved to be in the nanomolar range, with an average sensitivity of 0.97, βME concentration in the range of 1–100 μM. Selectivity tests indicated that the designed sensor performs specific in comparison with other thiol compounds.

The novel Cu-CDs, demonstrated an accurate, sensitive, selective, simple, and facile nanosensor for thiols detection. Using CDs as an appropriate biocompatible and environmental-friendly fluorescent probe was very promising. Furthermore, since manufacturing CDs is very cost-effective, and scince the sample preparation and testing routine is quite easy, this sensing platform has many privileges in comparison with the conventional measurement methods.

The electrochemical peak at about zero potential of Cu-CDs was another major advantage of the designed nanosensor, which can be selectively, rationally and intensively suppressed by thiols, due to the stable interaction between the functional groups of the Cu-CDs and -SH groups of thiols. it also assures that at low and zero potential the regular interfering components would not be oxidized.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
